stimulus:
Dissolution rate (%) of selected minerals in glacial meltwater, by glacier flow speed

MineralSLW (slow)MOD (slow)FAS (fast)EXP (fast)
Calcite22%26%84%91%
Gypsum31%33%72%88%
Dolomite17%20%65%78%

Researchers measuring chemical weathering in alpine catchments classified glacial meltwater by flow speed into four categories: SLW and MOD (both slow) and FAS and EXP (both fast). For any given mineral, the dissolution rate in slow-flowing SLW or MOD water was lower than the dissolution rate for that same mineral in faster-flowing FAS or EXP water. For instance, the dissolution rate of calcite in MOD meltwater was 26% while ______
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the example?

answerOptions.content: the dissolution rate of dolomite in FAS meltwater was 65%.
the dissolution rate of calcite in SLW meltwater was 22%.
the dissolution rate of gypsum in EXP meltwater was 88%.
the dissolution rate of calcite in FAS meltwater was 84%.

rationale:
Choice D is correct. The example sets up a comparison for one mineral (calcite) between a slow-flowing category and a fast-flowing one. The first half cites calcite in MOD (slow) at 26%; to illustrate the slow-vs.-fast pattern, the second half must give calcite in either FAS or EXP. The table shows calcite in FAS at 84%, much higher than 26%, which neatly illustrates the pattern.

Choice A is incorrect: it switches to a different mineral (dolomite), so it doesn't continue the comparison for calcite. Choice B is incorrect: it compares two slow-flow categories (MOD and SLW)—the example needs a slow-vs.-fast contrast. Choice C is incorrect: it switches to a different mineral (gypsum), so it doesn't complete the calcite example.
